items.title: Spain or Argentina, who's going to win? Europe has its say.
items.standfirst: Guess who the Brussels bubble thinks will triumph in the FIFA World Cup final.
items.content: Good morning and welcome to World Cup final day. POLITICO's live blog will be humming from now through the final whistle in New Jersey this afternoon. Let's start here ... POLITICO polled the great and the good of the Brussels bubble to hear which team they think will win the World Cup final on Sunday. And there’s not much love for Argentina. Glenn Micallef, European commissioner for sport: 2-0 Spain. Magnus Brunner, European commissioner for internal affairs and migration: “Both [teams] almost lost to Austria (just kidding), but the strong historical Austria-Spain connection compels me to say 2-1 for Spain. And because I hope an EU country wins!” Paula Pinho, European Commission chief spokesperson: “1-1 in regulation time, and Spain wins on penalties.” Jüri Laas, spokesperson for European Parliament President Roberta Metsola: “Hoping for positive and entertaining football. Probably a close game. Europe wins. Spain 2, Argentina 1.” Marcos Alonso Alonso, Spanish ambassador to the EU: “We’re sure that La Roja will bring joy to Europe.” Fernando Iglesias, Argentina’s ambassador to the EU: “May the final be the best, and may the best win the final (I mean: Messi).” Thomas Regnier, European Commission spokesperson and former professional footballer: “Spain wins 2-1. Messi scores his last World Cup goal from the spot.” Pieter Jan Kleiweg de Zwaan, Dutch ambassador to the EU: “It has to be Spain. 2-0. Argentina has been so lucky and it has to be a European country.” Roxana Mînzatu, executive vice president of the European Commission: “Looking forward to the Messi-Yamal duel and expecting an exciting match. Too close to call.” Christophe Hansen, European commissioner for agriculture: “Spain will be the first country in history to win the World Cup only conceding a single goal on the way. 2-0.” Ignazio Cocchiere, European Commission official and former professional footballer: “I say 2-1 Spain. The UEFA European champions against the Copa America and World Cup holders, you can’t ask for a better final. Spanish captain Rodri to be player of the match.” Edouard Philippe, former French PM, mayor of Le Havre and presidential candidate: “3-1 pour l’Espagne.” Carrie Budoff Brown, POLITICO Europe’s executive editor: “3-2 to Argentina. Messi pulls it out.”

items.title: Russia launches major ballistic missile attack on Ukrainian cities
items.standfirst: Eight people die and dozens are injured as several cities are hit by missile and drone attacks, while Kyiv continues its own assault on Russian infrastructure.
items.content: Eight people die and dozens are injured as several cities are hit by missile and drone attacks, while Kyiv continues its own assault on Russian infrastructure.

items.title: Russia hits Kyiv with ballistic missiles
items.standfirst: Russia has launched one of its largest ballistic missile attacks of the conflict in Ukraine. At least one person was killed and 16 others were injured after around two dozen ballistic missiles struck Kyiv overnight. Witnesses described charred apartment buildings with windows blown out, mangled debris, and overturned cars scattered across the city. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said six districts of the capital were hit. France 24 correspondent Gulliver Cragg reports from Kyiv.
items.content: Russia has launched one of its largest ballistic missile attacks of the conflict in Ukraine. At least one person was killed and 16 others were injured after around two dozen ballistic missiles struck Kyiv overnight. Witnesses described charred apartment buildings with windows blown out, mangled debris, and overturned cars scattered across the city. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said six districts of the capital were hit. France 24 correspondent Gulliver Cragg reports from Kyiv.

items.title: Russia launches deadly strikes on Kyiv after attack on Moscow warehouses
items.standfirst: Russia fired two dozen ballistic missiles at Kyiv overnight on Sunday, killing one person and wounding 16, in an ever-escalating air war that is taking an increasing civilian toll on both sides. The latest with France 24 correspondent Emmanuelle Chaze, reporting from Kyiv.
items.content: Russia fired two dozen ballistic missiles at Kyiv overnight on Sunday, killing one person and wounding 16, in an ever-escalating air war that is taking an increasing civilian toll on both sides. The latest with France 24 correspondent Emmanuelle Chaze, reporting from Kyiv.

items.title: Russia pounds Kyiv for five hours in one of its biggest ballistic missile attacks
items.standfirst: Attack on Ukraine’s capital and other cities leaves at least six people dead and dozens injuredRussia has carried out one of its biggest ballistic missile attacks on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, launching a five-hour assault that left at least six people dead and dozens injured, and caused fires and damage across the capital.Ukrainian officials said the capital had been hit with about 40 Iskander-M and hypersonic Zircon missiles. Residents heard an air raid siren sound at 1.30am. There was the sound of air defences, followed minutes later by a series of booms and explosions. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Cuban dissident artist arrives in US after 5-years in prison
clusters.neutral_headline: Cuban dissident Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara goes into exile in US
bullet_summary: Otero Alcántara co-founded the San Isidro Movement, a collective of Havana-based artists, writers, and musicians that protests against the Cuban government
Reported by France24, BBC World, NPR News

items.title: Cuban dissident artist arrives in US after 5-years in prison
items.standfirst: A hero's welcome for Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara as he arrived in Miami following his release from prison in Cuba. The dissident artist accepted exile after the United States granted him humanitarian parole. Otero Alcántara co-founded the San Isidro Movement, a collective of Havana-based artists, writers, and musicians that protests against the Cuban government. In 2021, he was arrested in Havana during anti-government demonstrations and was later sentenced to five years in prison on charges of public disorder, contempt, and disrespect for national symbols. Carla Colomé Santiago, a Cuban journalist based in Miami, shares her insights.
items.content: A hero's welcome for Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara as he arrived in Miami following his release from prison in Cuba. The dissident artist accepted exile after the United States granted him humanitarian parole. Otero Alcántara co-founded the San Isidro Movement, a collective of Havana-based artists, writers, and musicians that protests against the Cuban government. In 2021, he was arrested in Havana during anti-government demonstrations and was later sentenced to five years in prison on charges of public disorder, contempt, and disrespect for national symbols. Carla Colomé Santiago, a Cuban journalist based in Miami, shares her insights.

items.title: Kuwait, Bahrain respond to Iranian attacks amid growing escalation
items.standfirst: The risk of a wider war in the Middle East is growing after authorities in Bahrain and Kuwait said they intercepted missiles and drones launched from Iran. Air raid sirens also sounded in Jordan's capital, Amman, while Israel said it detected Iranian missiles targeting the coastal city of Aqaba. The escalation comes after the US Embassy in Jordan urged American citizens, in a social media post, to avoid Aqaba's airport and seaport because of a specific and credible threat. Teaching Fellow in US politics and international security at University of Portsmouth, Dafydd Townley, shares his insights.
items.content: The risk of a wider war in the Middle East is growing after authorities in Bahrain and Kuwait said they intercepted missiles and drones launched from Iran. Air raid sirens also sounded in Jordan's capital, Amman, while Israel said it detected Iranian missiles targeting the coastal city of Aqaba. The escalation comes after the US Embassy in Jordan urged American citizens, in a social media post, to avoid Aqaba's airport and seaport because of a specific and credible threat. Teaching Fellow in US politics and international security at University of Portsmouth, Dafydd Townley, shares his insights.

items.title: ICE officers to use body cameras during vehicle stops, Trump ‘border czar’ says
items.standfirst: Tom Homan’s comments come amid scrutiny over federal immigration agents’ fatal shooting of two men in six daysUS Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) will require its officers to record vehicle stops with at least one body camera, said Tom Homan, the Trump administration “border czar”, on Sunday. The action comes as the agency is under renewed scrutiny after ICE officers fatally shot two men earlier this month.“They exonerate more law enforcement than they convict, and I want officers to wear body cameras because I want the American people to see what the ⁠officers saw when they took that action,” Homan said on the Fox & Friends Weekend program. Continue reading...

The action comes as the agency is under renewed scrutiny after ICE officers fatally shot two men earlier this month.“They exonerate more law enforcement than they convict, and I want officers to wear body cameras because I want the American people to see what the ⁠officers saw when they took that action,” Homan said on the Fox & Friends Weekend program. items.title: Becker: "Hardly a week goes by" without Trump administration threatening election official arrests
items.standfirst: CBS News contributors David Becker and Chris Krebs joined "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" in the wake of President Trump's primetime speech last week accusing China of accessing election data. Meanwhile, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin said that if election officials do not secure elections, they could face fines or "depending on how far it goes, prison time." Becker said "hardly a week goes by where someone in this administration doesn't threaten election officials with arrest … it's become almost a running joke."
items.content: CBS News contributors David Becker and Chris Krebs joined "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" in the wake of President Trump's primetime speech last week accusing China of accessing election data. Meanwhile, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin said that if election officials do not secure elections, they could face fines or "depending on how far it goes, prison time." Becker said "hardly a week goes by where someone in this administration doesn't threaten election officials with arrest … it's become almost a running joke."

items.title: Trump border czar says "I think body cameras are the way to go" after deadly ICE shootings
items.standfirst: Amid questions about immigration agents' actions in deadly shootings this month in Texas and Maine, Trump administration border czar Tom Homan told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that "I think body cameras are the way to go," adding that body cameras "let the American people know what that officer sees when he takes that action."
items.content: Amid questions about immigration agents' actions in deadly shootings this month in Texas and Maine, Trump administration border czar Tom Homan told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that "I think body cameras are the way to go," adding that body cameras "let the American people know what that officer sees when he takes that action."

items.title: Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan says "affordability is a challenge" for customers
items.standfirst: Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that he has been hearing from customers that "affordability is a challenge" amid rising gas and food prices and inflation, but he added that "in the last couple months, we're seeing the wage growth of all income cohorts kind of coalesce together around 3 to 4% which is good."
items.content: Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that he has been hearing from customers that "affordability is a challenge" amid rising gas and food prices and inflation, but he added that "in the last couple months, we're seeing the wage growth of all income cohorts kind of coalesce together around 3 to 4% which is good."

items.title: Sen. Mark Warner says Middle East bases are "on and off under attack" since Iran war began
items.standfirst: In the wake of the deaths of two U.S. service members over the weekend at a Jordanian military base, Democratic Sen. Mark Warner of Virginia told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that "these bases have been on and off under attack" since the beginning of the war of Iran, which he described as a "war of choice."
items.content: In the wake of the deaths of two U.S. service members over the weekend at a Jordanian military base, Democratic Sen. Mark Warner of Virginia told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that "these bases have been on and off under attack" since the beginning of the war of Iran, which he described as a "war of choice."

items.title: Gov. Kathy Hochul says New York "has to lead" on 1-year, large-scale data center ban
items.standfirst: New York Gov. Kathy Hochul told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that she reversed her position on data centers because "the state has to lead," signing an executive order that banned large-scale data centers for one year to further study the impacts.
items.content: New York Gov. Kathy Hochul told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that she reversed her position on data centers because "the state has to lead," signing an executive order that banned large-scale data centers for one year to further study the impacts.

items.title: 7/19: Face The Nation
items.standfirst: This week on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," as the U.S. struck back at Iran overnight, Sen. Mark Warner joins to discuss the latest in the Middle East. Plus, CBS News contributors Chris Krebs and David Becker discuss election security.
items.content: This week on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," as the U.S. struck back at Iran overnight, Sen. Mark Warner joins to discuss the latest in the Middle East. Plus, CBS News contributors Chris Krebs and David Becker discuss election security.

items.title: Josh Kerr breaks 27-year-old mile record in front of packed London stadium
items.standfirst: British runner Josh Kerr has broken the 27-year-old world record for the mile in front of a sell-out crowd in London.

items.title: Hunger striker taken to hospital as police launch 'chilling crackdown'
items.standfirst: Police have forcibly removed hunger striker Sonam Wangchuk from a student protest site in India.
items.content: Police have forcibly removed hunger striker Sonam Wangchuk from a student protest site in India.
